name: Butterfree
route: Flower fields, forest canopies, berry groves, wet meadows, honey trees, rain-washed clearings, and migration corridors above old Caterpie nurseries
habitat: Flower-rich forests and meadows with clean water, high pollen diversity, tall roosting branches, low pesticide load, and safe wind corridors
behaviorTemperament: Gentle when unstressed, but easily startled into defensive powder release. Adults are focused on nectar, honey, mating flights, and finding safe egg plants.
diet: Flower nectar, honey, pollen, fruit juices, tree sap, mineral-rich water, and trace salts from damp soil
activityPattern: Diurnal, with peak activity during warm sun after rain. May continue short flights at dusk during migration or breeding season.
bestTimeToFind: Late morning in flower patches after rain, near honey trees, or above forests where Metapod clusters recently emerged
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Easy to see in flight, but difficult to approach without triggering powder release or sudden climbing flight.
conservationStatus: Important pollinator species under local protection in many forest reserves
populationStatus: Uncommon but locally abundant during emergence flights. Populations drop sharply when Caterpie host plants are sprayed or flower corridors are cleared.
description: A winged Bug/Flying-type Pokémon with compound eyes, rain-repellent wing scales, and poisonous defensive powder. It looks delicate, but a frightened Butterfree can turn a quiet clearing into a respiratory hazard.
lore: Villages near old Kanto forests used to hang white cloth outside before honey season. If the cloth turned silver with wing dust overnight, children were kept indoors until the Butterfree migration passed.
survivalUse: Can lead observers to flowers, honey, clean water, and healthy forest edges. Its presence suggests a complete insect life cycle nearby, from leaf plants to pollinator routes.
handlingWarning: Do not net roughly, grab wings, shake loose scales, or approach with open food near the face. Wing powder can irritate eyes, lungs, and skin, especially in children or people with asthma.
encounterAdvice: Stay downwind, move slowly, and avoid blocking escape routes. If the wings begin rapid shivering, step back before powder is released.
firstAid: For powder exposure, leave the area, remove contaminated outer clothing, rinse eyes and skin with clean water, and seek medical help for wheezing, swelling, confusion, or persistent coughing.
companionSuitability: Good for experienced gentle handlers with pollen-safe housing and respiratory precautions
fieldBehaviors: Seeks honey and nectar over long distances
Rubs pollen and honey onto leg hairs
Flies during rain because wing powder repels water
Uses compound eyes to detect tiny flower patches and distant movement
signsOfPresence: Fine powder on flowers or bark
Clusters of eggs under host leaves
Scattered toxic scales after predator attacks
Sudden quiet among smaller insects during defensive dusting
equipment: Respirator or dust mask for dense swarms
fieldNote: A calm Butterfree drifts between flowers. A frightened one beats its wings in place. If the air begins to sparkle, stop breathing deeply and leave the powder cloud.
originStory: Butterfree is the reward for surviving the Silk-Hunger Lineage, but not a peaceful reward. The same plants Caterpie devoured become toxins stored in wing scales. The same immobility Metapod endured becomes sudden flight. Adults carry forests forward as pollinators while leaving a warning in their wake: beauty is not safety. The powder that lets Butterfree escape predators can blind small animals, sicken humans, and mark a trail of glittering contamination after a panic flight.
domesticationStatus: Semi-domesticated as a pollinator companion and early-route partner
prey: Does not hunt vertebrate prey. Feeds on nectar, honey, pollen, fruit juice, sap, and mineral water.
territoryRange: Daily nectar route may cover several miles. Strong adults can locate flowers far from the nest and return by scent, light, and landmark memory.
lifeCycle: Emerges when Pokemon-Metapod evolves starting at level 10. Adults mate, pollinate flowering plants, place eggs on host leaves, and may access Pokemon-Gigantamax-Butterfree under rare special conditions.
containmentRequirements: Large flight enclosure, soft mesh walls, nectar and honey stations, clean flowering plants, rain-mist area, no wing-damaging fans, no pesticides, and respiratory-safe handling protocol
ecologicalRole: Pollinates flowers and berry plants, disperses pollen over long distances, helps indicate clean forest corridors, and links Caterpie host plants to meadow ecosystems.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Wing mites, scale rot, fungal wing damage, pollen mold, parasitic fly larvae, and respiratory powder buildup in confined colonies are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Adults lay eggs on selected host leaves near reliable nectar routes. Females avoid plants already marked by too many larvae or predator scent.
predators: Pidgeotto, Fearow, Zubat at dusk, Ariados, hungry reptile Pokémon, humans collecting wing scales, and larger airborne predators
emergencyResponse: For powder cloud indoors, evacuate sensitive people, open ventilation, avoid sweeping dry powder into the air, wet-wipe surfaces, and isolate the Butterfree in a calm dark carrier.
legalStatus: Pollinator protection rules may limit capture. Permits may be required for large swarms, breeding colonies, or public demonstrations because of airborne powder risk.
lifespan: Wild: 3 to 4 years in safe habitat, often much less under predator or pesticide pressure. Managed care: up to 5 years with stable flight space.
socialStructure: Seasonal loose swarms around flowers and breeding routes. Not a hive species, but adults tolerate one another when nectar is abundant.

fieldCapabilities.powderRange: Small defensive clouds 5 to 20 ft, wider downwind in dry air
fieldCapabilities.flightSpeed: Fast short evasive flight with strong hovering bursts
fieldCapabilities.rainFlight: Good due to water-repellent wing scales
fieldCapabilities.visualAcuity: High compound-eye motion and flower detection
fieldCapabilities.camouflage: Low in open air, moderate among flowers and dappled forest light

trainingRisk: Moderate. The main risks are respiratory exposure, wing injury, panic powder release, and accidental colony collapse from bad enclosure plants.
physiology: Butterfree has large compound eyes, delicate wings covered in water-repellent and toxic scales, strong flight muscles, and specialized leg hairs for carrying honey and pollen. Its final form converts the vulnerable larval food web into mobility, chemical defense, and pollination work.

keyFacts.diet: Nectar, honey, pollen, sap, fruit juice, and mineral water
keyFacts.habitat: Flower fields, forests, meadows, and berry groves
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: often 3 to 4 years in safe habitat
keyFacts.groupSize: Solitary, pairs, or seasonal loose swarms

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. Bonding is possible, but wing health, powder control, and clean nectar sources require steady care.
physicalCharacteristics: White body, large veined wings, purple limbs, red compound eyes, black antennae, fine wing scales, and small gripping feet adapted for flowers and branches.
dietRestrictions: Avoid pesticide-treated flowers, moldy honey, dyed sugar water, smoke-contaminated nectar, greasy human food, artificial fragrance sprays, and flowers from roadsides or chemical-treated lawns.

pokenatomyInspiration.pages: Pokemon-Butterfree anatomy and profile pages 34-35 of the uploaded Pokenatomy PDF

evolutionLine.name: Pokemon-Caterpie
evolutionLine.method: Wild hatchling or egg. Evolves into Pokemon-Metapod starting at level 7.
evolutionLine.note: Soft-bodied larval stage with suction feet, silk production, and a defensive odor organ.

evolutionLine.name: Pokemon-Metapod
evolutionLine.method: Evolves from Pokemon-Caterpie starting at level 7. Evolves into Pokemon-Butterfree starting at level 10.
evolutionLine.note: Immobile chrysalis stage. The shell hardens while the body inside breaks down and rebuilds for flight.

evolutionLine.name: Pokemon-Butterfree
evolutionLine.method: Evolves from Pokemon-Metapod starting at level 10. Gigantamax form requires the Gigantamax factor.
evolutionLine.note: Final Bug/Flying pollinator stage with compound eyes, water-repellent toxic wing powder, and honey-seeking behavior.

evolutionTree.subtitle: Evolution Tree - Silk and Powder Line

mainLine.name: Pokemon-Caterpie
mainLine.note: Soft prey-stage larva that survives by eating constantly, climbing well, making silk, and releasing foul odor from its antenna.

mainLine.name: Pokemon-Metapod
mainLine.note: Chrysalis stage with little mobility. The shell is armor, but the forming body inside is dangerously tender.

mainLine.name: Pokemon-Butterfree
mainLine.note: Winged adult that carries pollen, honey, and toxic defensive powder through forest canopies and rainstorms.

target.name: Pokemon-Gigantamax-Butterfree
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat wing scales and powder clouds as area contamination hazards.

originContinuity.coreMutation: A forest caterpillar line developed hyper-efficient gut chambers, defensive scent glands, and silk scaffolding that allowed sudden, energy-heavy metamorphosis into a flying adult.
originContinuity.sharedDarkTheme: The line survives by accepting three different kinds of weakness: Caterpie is eaten by nearly everything, Metapod must become helpless while its body rebuilds, and Butterfree survives by filling the air with powder that can save it while harming everything nearby.
stageProgression: Pokemon-Caterpie: hunger is the survival engine. The larva eats until its skin can no longer contain it.
Pokemon-Metapod: the body becomes a sealed workshop. A living animal dissolves into tissue that can be rebuilt into wings.
Pokemon-Butterfree: the adult escapes the food chain by becoming airborne, toxic, and useful to forests as a pollinator.
originContinuity.fieldMyth: Old Kanto rangers call mass Metapod clusters 'green graves' because a healthy grove can look like it is full of hanging shells, and only some of them ever open from the inside.

sourceNotes: Official Pokémon Pokédex data used for typing, category, height, weight, weakness, ability, descriptions, and evolution family.
PokémonDB data used for level-based evolution, hidden abilities, base stats, breeding data, and detailed type defenses.
Pokenatomy: An Unofficial Guide used as dark anatomical inspiration for Caterpie silk and odor organs, Metapod chrysalis vulnerability, and Butterfree toxic wing powder and pollination behavior.
Dark origin material is original fiction created for the Field Pokédex and is not official Pokémon canon.
Naming convention uses Pokemon-XXX for file names, ids, detailPath values, index names, and evolution node names. displayName and commonName keep the readable Pokémon name.
